justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) CQRS correctly identified as appropriate with clear reasoning about read/write asymmetry (500 writes/day vs complex analytical reads across 50M rows). (2) Read/write model separation clearly designed — normalized write model unchanged, four denormalized projection tables for reads. (3) Eventual consistency trade-off explicitly stated and quantified for the use case (single-digit seconds lag, acceptable for operational reporting). (4) 50M record migration risk identified as high severity with concrete mitigation (batch migration script, maintenance window). (5) Implementation guidance is highly actionable — three phases, specific file paths, SQL table definitions, per-projection handlers. (6) CQRS correctly limited to read path only — explicitly calls out NOT implementing event sourcing or write-side CQRS in pitfalls_to_avoid.

observations.text: Exceptionally detailed implementation guidance with specific file paths, migration scripts, and a three-phase rollout plan — goes well beyond minimum pass criteria
Correctly avoids recommending event sourcing, explicitly calling it out as a pitfall to avoid
Transactional outbox pattern is a pragmatic, well-reasoned synchronization choice for the 500/day throughput — avoids over-engineering with Kafka/RabbitMQ
Projection drift risk identified with three-layer defense: transactional outbox, idempotent consumers, periodic reconciliation
Eventual consistency quantified specifically for this use case — single-digit seconds at 500 events/day, with practical UX mitigation (last-updated indicator)
Four specific denormalized projection tables named and mapped to dashboard queries — concrete denormalization guidance for the 12-table write model
Flexibility and rigidity points show mature architectural thinking — evolution paths for scaling beyond current requirements without over-building now

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: CQRS correctly identified as appropriate with strong reasoning (read/write asymmetry, 500 writes/day vs 50M records, normalized schema mismatch). Read/write model separation clearly designed with four specific projection tables. Eventual consistency trade-off explicitly stated and quantified for the use case (seconds to minutes, acceptable at 500 writes/day). 50M record migration risk identified with concrete mitigation (chunked batch job, off-peak, idempotent). Implementation guidance is highly actionable for Shaq with specific files, table names, and step-by-step phases. CQRS correctly limited to read path — explicitly states write model remains normalized and unchanged, and explicitly warns against event sourcing and write-side complexity.

observations.text: Explicitly rejects event sourcing, message brokers, and separate databases as unnecessary for 500 writes/day — demonstrates restraint and practical judgment
Four specific projection tables mapped to dashboard views (orders_by_status, delivery_success_rates, transit_time_by_zone, courier_rankings) with concrete file paths
Eventual consistency quantified: sub-second to seconds at 0.35 writes/minute, with recommendation to show 'last updated' timestamps in UI
50M backfill addressed with chunked processing, idempotency, and dual-purpose as rebuild capability
Pitfalls section directly addresses all failure modes from the rubric — no write-side CQRS, no event sourcing, no over-engineering
Trade-offs section is exceptionally thorough with alternatives considered for each decision
Projection drift risk identified as high severity with full rebuild capability as mitigation — shows operational maturity

trials.justification: All rubric criteria met: CQRS correctly identified with strong reasoning (read/write asymmetry, 12-table joins, 50M rows); read/write separation clearly designed with write model unchanged and four denormalized read tables; eventual consistency explicitly stated and quantified (<5s lag, acceptable for operational dashboard); 50M record migration risk identified as high severity with concrete mitigation (off-peak batch job, read replicas, 2-4 hour estimate); implementation guidance is highly actionable with phased timeline, specific file paths, table names, and concrete patterns; CQRS correctly limited to read path only with explicit warnings against event sourcing and write-side CQRS.

observations.text: Explicitly and repeatedly rejects event sourcing as unnecessary complexity, correctly scoping CQRS to the read side only
Transactional outbox pattern is a pragmatic choice for 500 writes/day, avoiding Kafka/RabbitMQ over-engineering
Names all four concrete denormalized read model tables mapped to specific dashboard queries
Quantifies eventual consistency lag at <5s and explains why this is acceptable for the use case
50M backfill risk identified as high severity with practical mitigation including time estimate
Reconciliation job included to detect projection drift — addresses a commonly missed operational concern
Implementation guidance includes phased weekly plan, specific files, migration SQL, and anti-patterns to avoid
Pitfalls section directly addresses all failure modes from the rubric: no Kafka, no ES, no write-side denormalization, no strong consistency chasing
